text: Verdict
Outcome
Ruling
Finding
Consequence
Aftermath
Conviction
Costs
Comply
Conditions
Redress
Seal
The evidence was so strong that a conviction followed.
It was such a serious offence that custody was likely.
I will explain the conditions so that you can comply.
The ruling was final; consequently, we must look at the appeal.
I heard the word guilty and everything stopped. What does the verdict actually mean for me?
Let me explain the outcome plainly. The evidence was so strong that the court reached a guilty finding, and the ruling stands.
So what is the consequence? Do I go to prison?
No. The conviction carries a record and redress to the victim, but the sentence is suspended; consequently, you serve no time if you comply with the conditions.
So this record follows me forever?
Not forever. The costs are real now, but if you comply for the full period, we can apply to seal it. I will write everything down so that the road ahead is clear.
The aftermath is so heavy that I could not think before. Now, at least, I understand it.

After a verdict, the hardest conversation is not about the law; it is about consequence. When you explain an outcome to a client, three things matter. First, name the outcome plainly. The court reached its finding, and the ruling stands, so the client must understand exactly what happened before anything else. Second, link every fact to its consequence. The evidence was strong, so the conviction followed; the offence was serious, so the costs are high. I use linkers like so that and as a result, because a client who sees the chain of cause and effect can accept it. Third, turn to the road ahead. There may be an appeal, and there may be conditions to comply with, such that life does not simply return to normal. I explain that the sentence carries conditions the client must meet, so that, in time, the record can be sealed. A good defender does not abandon the client at the verdict; the work continues, so that the outcome, hard as it is, becomes something the client can live with and move beyond.

You are listening to the legal affairs review, with a feature this week on what happens after the verdict. Researchers who study the criminal courts say the moment a ruling is delivered is so emotional that clients often hear almost nothing of what follows. As a result, experts urge defenders to slow down and explain the outcome in plain language. One veteran defender told us she always links each fact to its consequence, so that the client can follow the reasoning rather than simply absorb the shock. The conviction had such serious consequences that her client could not take them in at first; consequently, she scheduled a second meeting to explain the costs and the conditions calmly. She also stressed the road ahead: an appeal may be possible, and if the conditions are met, the record may one day be sealed. The message from the panel was clear: a verdict is not the end of a defender's duty. Explaining the outcome, so that the client truly understands it, is part of the job.
